department_leader: Bot 7 — Food Allergy and Intolerance Assessment
key_learnings: Structured meal planning reduces food waste by up to 23% and measurably improves overall diet quality scores. Meal frequency — whether 3 or 6 meals per day — has minimal effect on total daily energy expenditure but significantly impacts hunger regulation and long-term dietary adherence. Batch cooking and weekly meal prep are the most frequently cited behavioral drivers of sustained dietary compliance.
research_findings: Randomized trials demonstrate that meal planning is associated with healthier food choices, greater dietary variety, and lower BMI over time. Time-restricted eating windows of 8–10 hours improve metabolic markers independently of overall caloric restriction. Front-loading caloric intake earlier in the day is associated with better weight management outcomes compared to evening-heavy eating patterns.
current_focus_areas: Building constraint-based meal plan generators that account for allergies, budget, and cooking time; developing circadian-aligned meal scheduling recommendations; integrating with Bots 5, 6, 7, and 9 for cross-module meal optimization; serving as the meal architecture hub within the Dietary Safety & Meal Architecture department.

learning_log.entry: Assigned to the Dietary Safety & Meal Architecture department under Department Leader Bot 7 (Food Allergy and Intolerance Assessment). Continuing collaborative meal planning research and reporting daily to Copilot. Progress: 55%.
